pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Bacterial pustule is caused by the bacterium  Xanthomonas axonopodis  pv.  glycines .

type_of_disease.quote: Bacterial pustule is caused by the bacterium  Xanthomonas axonopodis  pv.  glycines .

affected_parts.quote: Symptoms begin as small, light green spots (not water-soaked) with raised centers on the upper and lower surfaces of leaves. ... Small raised spots may also develop on pods.

summary.value: Symptoms begin as small, light green to yellowish spots (not water-soaked) with raised centers on the upper and lower surfaces of leaves, with light-colored pustules (blisters) developing in the center of lesions, especially on the undersides; lesions may coalesce into large necrotic areas, giving leaves a ragged appearance and possibly causing premature defoliation.
summary.quote: Symptoms begin as small, light green spots (not water-soaked) with raised centers on the upper and lower surfaces of leaves. Light-colored pustules (blisters) often develop in the center of lesions, especially on the undersides of leaves.

diagnostic_features.value: Differentiated from bacterial blight by a lack of water soaking at the initial site of infection, less chlorosis around lesions, and by the formation of tiny tan bumps on the undersides of leaves. Unlike soybean rust, bacterial pustule does not have an opening in the pustules or masses of spores.
diagnostic_features.quote: Bacterial pustule can be differentiated by a lack of water soaking at the initial site of infection, less chlorosis around lesions, and by the formation of tiny tan bumps on the undersides of leaves. The pustules can be mistaken for those of soybean rust, however, bacterial pustule does not have an opening in the pustules or masses of spores like those of soybean rust.

look_alikes.quote: Early symptoms of bacterial pustule look similar to bacterial blight. ... The pustules can be mistaken for those of soybean rust

value: Plowing may be beneficial in fields where bacterial pustule is common
Avoid field cultivation when the foliage is wet
Foliar fungicides are not effective against this bacterial disease
treatments.quote: Plant resistant varieties and use pathogen-free seed to help manage bacterial pustule. Rotate soybeans with non-host crops. Plowing may be beneficial in fields where bacterial pustule is common. Avoid field cultivation when the foliage is wet.

conflicts.note: Cornell CALS lists only foliar; UMN Extension reports raised spots on pods as well. Canonical value uses the more complete UMN description.

conflicts.canonical_value: Plant resistant varieties; pathogen-free seed; crop rotation; plowing; avoid wet cultivation
conflicts.note: Cornell CALS states the disease rarely reaches epidemic levels in NY and requires no management; UMN Extension recommends active cultural controls. Both are preserved.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Bacterial blight is caused by  Pseudomonas syringae  pv.  glycinea

type_of_disease.quote: Pseudomonas syringae pv. glycinea is the causal agent of bacterial blight in soybean. This pathogen survives in crop residue and can be seed transmitted.

affected_parts.quote: Bacterial blight can occur on all above ground plant parts, but is most evident on leaves in the mid to upper canopy. ... Infection can also occur on stems, petioles, pods, and seeds in infected pods.

summary.value: Infections begin as small, angular, water-soaked spots that turn yellow and then brown as tissue dies; spots darken and are surrounded by yellowish-green halos and often merge into large, dead patches, with dead tissue falling out to give leaves a ragged appearance.
summary.quote: Infections begin as small, angular, water-soaked spots that turn yellow and then brown as the tissue dies. The spots darken and are surrounded by yellowish-green halos. Spots often merge to form large, dead patches on the leaves. The dead tissue may fall out giving the leaves a ragged appearance.

diagnostic_features.value: Distinguished from Septoria brown spot by the presence of a yellow halo around lesions and by occurring on young leaves in the mid to upper canopy (whereas brown spot is usually seen on older, lower leaves); lacks fungal structures in lesions, and leaf veins may become affected creating necrotic areas resembling lightning on the leaf.
diagnostic_features.quote: Bacterial blight closely resembles brown spot ( Septoria glycines ), but can be distinguished by the lack of fungal structures in lesions, and the presence of large yellow halos around the lesions. Leaf veins may become affected and create necrotic areas resembling lightning on the leaf.

look_alikes.quote: Bacterial blight can be mistaken for Septoria brown spot.

value: Avoid field cultivation when the foliage is wet
Deep tillage may help in the rare fields where this disease is frequently severe
treatments.quote: Plant resistant soybean varieties and rotate with non-host crops. Always use pathogen-free seed. Field cultivation should be avoided when the foliage is wet. Deep tillage may help in the rare fields where this disease is frequently severe.

conflicts.note: NC State Extension only notes foliar symptoms and seed transmission; UMN Extension also reports infection on stems, petioles, and pods. Canonical value uses the more complete UMN description.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Bean pod mottle is caused by the comovirus Bean pod mottle virus.

type_of_disease.quote: Bean pod mottle is caused by the comovirus Bean pod mottle virus.

affected_parts.quote: Symptoms of BPMV are green to yellow mottling (blotchiness) of younger leaves in the upper canopy. In severe cases leaves may show puckering and distortion and plants can be stunted. Pods can be misshapen with mottling and reduced in size. Infected plants may have green stems after the pods have matured. Seed from infected plants may appear smoky in color or have reduced size and quality due to seed coat mottling which will be brown or black depending on hilum color.

summary.value: Green to yellow mottling (blotchiness) of younger leaves in the upper canopy, with possible puckering and distortion of leaves and stunting of plants; pods may be misshapen with mottling and reduced size, and seeds may appear smoky or have seed coat mottling.
summary.quote: Symptoms of BPMV are green to yellow mottling (blotchiness) of younger leaves in the upper canopy. In severe cases leaves may show puckering and distortion and plants can be stunted. Pods can be misshapen with mottling and reduced in size. Infected plants may have green stems after the pods have matured. Seed from infected plants may appear smoky in color or have reduced size and quality due to seed coat mottling which will be brown or black depending on hilum color.

diagnostic_features.value: Mild chlorotic mottling to severe mosaic on younger leaves in the upper canopy, green stem after pod maturity, leaf distortion and puckering; symptom development favored by cool temperatures and may not be apparent in higher temperatures or after pod set; seed coat mottling brown or black depending on hilum color.
diagnostic_features.quote: Symptom development is favored by cool temperatures, but may not be apparent in higher temperatures or after pod set. Symptoms first appear on the younger leaves and can range from mild chlorotic mottling of foliage to a severe mosaic

look_alikes.quote: Higher yield losses can be observed if plants are infected on or before the V6 stage or co-infections with Soybean mosaic virus .

value: Insecticide seed treatments and foliar sprays targeting management of the bean leaf beetle will reduce the incidence of BPMV
Early season management action (most damage is from early season introduction)
Managing weed hosts within fields may also reduce reservoirs for virus
treatments.quote: Cultural practices such as late planting will reduce BPMV... Insecticide seed treatments and foliar sprays targeting management of the bean leaf beetle will reduce the incidence of BPMV. Remember that most of the damage is from early season introduction, so trying to manage this disease during the pod filling stages will not have as much impact as an early season management action.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Cercospora leaf blight is caused by the fungus  Cercospora kikuchii .

type_of_disease.quote: Cercospora leaf blight is caused by the fungus  Cercospora kikuchii .

affected_parts.quote: Reddish-purple lesions may also occur on petioles, stems, and pods.

summary.value: Light purple spots and areas develop on the top surface of leaves exposed to light, deepening to reddish-purple or bronze and giving leaves a leathery, 'sunburned' appearance, with reddish-purple lesions also on petioles, stems, and pods; symptoms typically appear on uppermost leaves during late reproductive stages (R5-R6).
summary.quote: The first symptom of Cercospora leaf blight is the development of light purple spots and areas on the top surface of leaves exposed to light. The discolored areas expand and deepen in color to a reddish-purple or bronze. The infected leaves appear leathery and 'sunburned'.

diagnostic_features.value: Purplish or bronzed discoloration on the uppermost, sun-exposed leaves giving a leathery, shiny 'sunburned' appearance, typically during late reproductive stages (R5-R6), with reddish-purple lesions on petioles, stems, and pods.
diagnostic_features.quote: The most distinctive symptom of CLB is the purplish or bronzed discoloration that appears on the uppermost leaves of infected plants, typically during the late reproductive stages (R5-R6). This discoloration gives the affected leaves a leathery, shiny appearance.

value: Use pathogen-free seed and plant partially resistant varieties with low susceptibility
Crop rotation with nonhost crops (such as corn or small grains)
Tillage to break down soybean residue and reduce inoculum
Foliar fungicide applications, particularly in fields with disease history
Regular scouting during reproductive stages for early detection
treatments.quote: Resistant Varieties: While there are no soybean varieties fully resistant to C. kikuchii , some varieties show partial resistance to CLB and purple seed stain. Planting these varieties can help reduce the severity of the disease. Crop Rotation and Residue Management: Since C. kikuchii survives in soybean residue, crop rotation with nonhost crops (such as corn or small grains) can help reduce inoculum levels in the field. Additionally, practices like tillage can help break down soybean residue, thereby reducing the amount of inoculum available to infect the next soybean crop. Fungicide Applications: Fungicides can be an effective tool for managing CLB, particularly in fields with a history of the disease... Scouting and Field Monitoring: Regular scouting is critical for early detection of CLB... Seed Quality Management: seed treatments may be necessary to protect seed lots intended for planting.

conflicts.note: NDSU describes CLB strictly as a foliar disease; UMN documents lesions on petioles, stems, and pods as well.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Frogeye leaf spot is caused by the fungus Cercospora sojina .

type_of_disease.quote: Frogeye leaf spot is caused by the fungus Cercospora sojina .

affected_parts.quote: Severe cases lead to premature leaf drop and can spread to stems and pods. The fungus that causes frogeye leaf spot may also cause purple petiole lesions (Figure 9) and infect seed.

summary.value: Leaf lesions are small, irregular to circular in shape, and gray with reddish-brown borders, occurring most commonly on the upper leaf surface and starting as dark, water-soaked spots; severe cases lead to premature leaf drop and can spread to stems and pods.
summary.quote: Leaf lesions are small, irregular to circular in shape, and gray with reddish-brown borders. Most commonly occurring on the upper leaf surface, lesions start as dark, water-soaked spots that vary in size.

diagnostic_features.value: As lesions age, the central area becomes gray to light brown with dark, red-brown margins; seed stain caused by C. sojina is light purple to gray (versus royal purple from CLB pathogens).
diagnostic_features.quote: As lesions age, the central area becomes gray to light brown with dark, red-brown margins.

look_alikes.quote: The foliar phase of FLS is usually severe and easily distinguishable from CLB when FLS petiole lesions occur.

value: Plant resistant soybean varieties (varieties with resistance to all known races are available)
Rotate to a non-host crop and use tillage to reduce survival of C. sojina (alfalfa, corn, and small grains are not susceptible)
Apply foliar fungicides during late flowering and early pod set to pod filling stages
treatments.quote: Resistant soybean varieties are available and should be used where disease is a potential problem. Several races of the pathogen have been identified, and varieties with resistance to all known races are available. Rotating to a non-host crop and tillage will reduce survival of C. sojina . Crops not susceptible to this pathogen are alfalfa, corn, and small grains. Foliar fungicides applied during late flowering and early pod set to pod filling stages can reduce frogeye leaf spot.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Downy mildew is caused by the oomycete Peronospora manshurica .

type_of_disease.quote: Downy mildew is caused by the oomycete Peronospora manshurica .

affected_parts.quote: Lesions occur on upper leaf surfaces as irregularly shaped, pale green to light yellow spots... Downy mildew-infected pods show no external symptoms, but the inside of the pod and seed may be covered with a dried, whitish fungal mass

summary.value: Irregularly shaped, pale green to light yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces that enlarge into pale to bright yellow spots, with older lesions turning brown with yellow-green margins; fuzzy gray tufts may appear on leaf undersides under high humidity.
summary.quote: Lesions occur on upper leaf surfaces as irregularly shaped, pale green to light yellow spots that enlarge into pale to bright yellow spots. Older lesions turn brown with yellow-green margins... On the leaf underside, fuzzy, gray tufts may be seen growing from lesions, particularly when humidity is high or leaves are wet

diagnostic_features.value: Fuzzy, gray tufts (sporulation) growing from lesions on the leaf underside, particularly when humidity is high or leaves are wet; infected pods have no external symptoms but the inside of pod and seed may be covered with a dried, whitish, crusty fungal mass.
diagnostic_features.quote: On the leaf underside, fuzzy, gray tufts may be seen growing from lesions, particularly when humidity is high or leaves are wet (e.g., early in the morning). Downy mildew-infected pods show no external symptoms, but the inside of the pod and seed may be covered with a dried, whitish fungal mass that appears crusty and contains spores.

look_alikes.quote: Some soybean varieties express mild leaf distortion that may resemble symptoms caused by common soybean viruses... Infected seed has a dull white appearance and is partially or completely covered with a pale coating of fungal spores which can be confused with white mold.

value: Plant resistant varieties (numerous sources of resistance are available)
Crop rotation and burial/management of infested crop residue to reduce pathogen levels
Plant clean, certified disease-free seed; do not plant seed from fields infected the previous year
Fungicides are generally not recommended for downy mildew management (most foliar fungicides are not labeled for downy mildew and would have very limited effect because the pathogen is an oomycete)
treatments.quote: The disease rarely reduces yield, so fungicides are not recommended for downy mildew management. Implementing cultural controls to reduce the risk of downy mildew is generally all that is needed. Plant clean seed . Growers planting non-GMO varieties should use certified, disease-free seed. Do not plant seed from fields infected the previous year. Rotate crops and manage crop residue . The downy mildew fungus survives in crop residue and on the surface of seed. Longer crop rotations and placing crop residues into contact with the soil after harvest will help reduce future infections. Resistant varieties . Because downy mildew is not considered to be yield limiting, most seed companies do not provide resistance ratings. However, numerous sources of resistance are available.

conflicts.note: SRIN describes downy mildew as 'a common fungal leaf disease', but Crop Protection Network and Cornell CALS both correctly identify the causal agent Peronospora manshurica as an oomycete (not a true fungus). Canonical value 'Oomycete' is supported by 2 of 3 sources and is taxonomically correct.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Charcoal rot is caused by the soilborne fungus  Macrophomina phaseolina .

type_of_disease.quote: Macrophomina phaseolina is an Ascomycete fungus.

affected_parts.quote: At the seedling stage, typical symptoms include , brown to dark spots on cotyledons. At the unifoliate leaf stage (VC), circular to oblong, reddish-brown, lesions that eventually turn dark brown to black may be observed. Mid to late season aboveground symptoms include light brown to grey superficial lesions in the stem. A characteristic sign of the pathogen on soybean is the presence of microsclerotia in the vascular tissue, in the pith, and mature and dry pods. Cross-sections of the lower stem and taproot may become streaked with gray hyphae and microsclerotia

summary.value: Infected plants are often wilted, stunted, and may die prematurely; leaflets become small and turn brown, while the taproot and lower stem develop light gray streaking with small black specks (microsclerotia) beneath the epidermis giving a charcoal-sprinkled or marbled appearance, and reddish-brown discoloration develops in the pith and vascular tissues.
summary.quote: Infected plants may die prematurely and are often wilted and stunted... After mid-season, leaflets on infected plants may be small and ultimately wilt and turn brown. The taproot and lower stem may become streaked with light gray. Small black specks may form beneath the epidermis and inside the lower stem and taproot to give them a charcoal-sprinkled appearance.

diagnostic_features.value: Presence of microsclerotia (small black specks) beneath the epidermis and inside the lower stem and taproot giving a charcoal-sprinkled or marbled appearance, with microsclerotia also in vascular tissue, pith, and mature dry pods; reddish-brown discoloration in pith and vascular tissues.
diagnostic_features.quote: A characteristic sign of the pathogen on soybean is the presence of microsclerotia in the vascular tissue, in the pith, and mature and dry pods. Cross-sections of the lower stem and taproot may become streaked with gray hyphae and microsclerotia, giving a marbled appearance

value: Manage fields to reduce or avoid drought stress, including irrigation when needed
Reduced tillage to keep soils cooler and reduce moisture stress
Crop rotation with non-host or low-susceptibility crops such as cereal grains
Reduced seeding rates to limit drought stress
Select cultivars with lower susceptibility (no fully resistant varieties available)
Fungicides are not typically recommended for managing charcoal rot
treatments.quote: Manage fields to reduce or avoid drought stress. Reduced tillage may reduce charcoal rot, perhaps due to cooler soils and less moisture stress. Rotation with crops that have relatively low susceptibility to charcoal rot, such as cereal grains, may be beneficial. Reduced seeding rates may also reduce drought stress and charcoal rot. Soybean cultivars may exhibit different levels of susceptibility, but none are fully resistant to this disease.

conflicts.canonical_value: Root, Stem, Foliar, Seed, Pod, Vascular
conflicts.note: UMN lists root, stem, foliar, and vascular involvement; NCSU additionally documents symptoms on cotyledons (seed) and microsclerotia in mature and dry pods.

conflicts.alternative_value: UMN does not address fungicide use; NCSU explicitly states fungicides are not typically recommended
conflicts.note: Sources align on cultural management; only NCSU comments on fungicide use.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Southern blight is caused by Agroathelia rolfsii , a soilborne fungus that has a wide host range of more than 200 plant species.

type_of_disease.quote: Southern blight is caused by Agroathelia rolfsii , a soilborne fungus that has a wide host range of more than 200 plant species.

affected_parts.quote: Seedling infection results in pre- or post-emergence damping-off. Later in the season, entire plants may become yellow and wilt, with leaves turning brown and often remaining attached to the plant. A dark brown lesion that girdles the stem occurs at the soil surface.

summary.value: Entire plants may become yellow and wilt with leaves turning brown and remaining attached, with a dark brown girdling lesion at the soil surface accompanied by white fanlike mats of fungal mycelium and small round sclerotia on the lower stem.
summary.quote: Later in the season, entire plants may become yellow and wilt, with leaves turning brown and often remaining attached to the plant. A dark brown lesion that girdles the stem occurs at the soil surface. This lesion is generally accompanied by the development of conspicuous white, fanlike mats of fungal mycelium that form on the base of the stem, on leaf residue, and on the soil surface around infected plants.

diagnostic_features.value: Conspicuous white, fanlike mats of fungal mycelium on the base of the stem and soil surface, with numerous small round sclerotia about the size of mustard seeds that progress from yellow-tan to reddish-brown to dark brown at maturity.
diagnostic_features.quote: This lesion is generally accompanied by the development of conspicuous white, fanlike mats of fungal mycelium that form on the base of the stem, on leaf residue, and on the soil surface around infected plants. Numerous, small round fungal bodies that are about the size of mustard seeds (called sclerotia) form on these fungal mats and on the lower stem. Initially, sclerotia are yellow-tan then progress to a reddish-brown color and finally dark brown at maturity.

value: Alternating soybean or other susceptible crops with nonhost crops such as maize, grain sorghum, wheat, or pasture grasses or clean fallow for 2 years may prevent the buildup of inoculum to damaging levels.
treatments.quote: Alternating soybean or other susceptible crops with nonhost crops such as maize, grain sorghum, wheat, or pasture grasses or clean fallow for 2 years may prevent the buildup of inoculum to damaging levels.

conflicts.note: Sclerotium rolfsii is the older/synonym name for the asexual stage; Agroathelia rolfsii (formerly Athelia rolfsii) is used by Crop Protection Network (APS-affiliated, higher authority rank). Taxonomy validation was not provided for this run.
UT Crops uses the dual common-name form referencing the older pathogen genus Sclerotium.
CPN reports seedling/whole-plant impact (damping-off) in addition to foliar/stem; UT Crops only describes stem and foliar symptoms.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Asian soybean rust, caused by the fungus Phakopsora pachyrhizi, is a soybean pathogen described for the first time in the Southern portion of North Carolina in 2005.

type_of_disease.quote: The causal agent is the basidiomycete fungus, Phakopsora pachyrhizi.

affected_parts.quote: Lesions tend to be angular with 2-5 mm diameter, and can affect not only leaves, but may be observed on petioles, pods, and stems ( Figure 1 ).

summary.value: Gray-green, tan to dark-brown, or reddish-brown angular lesions (2-5 mm) develop first on the underside of leaves in the lower canopy, with small pustules (blisters) that break open and release masses of tan spores; lesions are associated with leaf chlorosis and may also occur on petioles, pods, and stems, with high lesion density causing defoliation and early maturity.
summary.quote: A common symptom of soybean rust is the formation of gray-green, tan to dark-brown, or reddish-brown lesions that may become erumpant under the lesions on leaflets. Lesions tend to be angular with 2-5 mm diameter, and can affect not only leaves, but may be observed on petioles, pods, and stems ( Figure 1 ). Lesions are associated with leaf chlorosis; high lesion density can cause defoliation and early maturity of the plant

diagnostic_features.value: Angular lesions 2-5 mm in diameter on leaflets that become erumpant with small pustules (blisters) that break open and release masses of tan urediniospores; visible with a 20X hand lens, accompanied by leaf chlorosis.
diagnostic_features.quote: Small pustules (blisters) develop in the lesions, which break open and release masses of tan spores. The lesions and pustules, which can be seen with a 20X hand lens, may also appear on pods and stems.

value: other common leaf diseases on soybean
look_alikes.quote: Early symptoms of soybean rust (before pustules develop) are difficult to distinguish from other common leaf diseases on soybean.

value: Scout the lower canopy of fields weekly, especially during wet weather after soybeans begin to flower
Soybean rust must be managed with the judicious use of fungicides applied properly and at the correct time
Fungicide applications made between R3 and R5 may provide the most yield protection
Use different modes of action if more than one fungicide is needed in a growing season (to manage fungicide resistance)
Fungicides are likely not needed if soybean rust is not within 100 miles of a field
Regularly scout fields throughout the growing season
Resistant soybean varieties are in development
treatments.quote: The primary management of rust is through the use of fungicide applications. Fungicide applications made between R3 and R5 may provide the most yield protection for soybeans. Several fungicides are effective at reducing damages caused by rust, and efficacy can be found on the NC State Soybean Extension portal . Fungicide resistance is a concern with foliar pathogens, and different modes of action should be used if more than one fungicide is needed in a growing season. Fungicides are likely not needed if soybean rust is not within 100 miles of a field. ... Regularly scouting fields throughout the growing season is also important to monitor for foliar diseases like rust.

conflicts.note: UMN uses the simpler 'Soybean rust'; NC State uses 'Asian soybean rust' to distinguish from the milder P. meibomiae form. Both refer to disease caused by P. pachyrhizi.
NC State explicitly adds petioles to the list of affected tissues; UMN omits petioles.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Phytophthora root and stem rot of soybean is caused by Phytophthora sojae .

type_of_disease.quote: P. sojae is not a true fungus, but belongs to a group of fungal like organisms called oomycetes (water molds).

affected_parts.quote: infected stems appear bruised and are soft, secondary roots are rotted, the leaves turn yellow, and brown and plants can wilt and die... Phytophthora can attack and rot seeds and seedling prior to or anytime after emergence.

summary.value: Occurs in wet, waterlogged, compacted soils with symptoms appearing during or shortly after waterlogged conditions. Plants may be stunted and chlorotic early in the disease cycle; infected stems appear bruised and soft, secondary roots are rotted, leaves turn yellow and brown, and plants can wilt and die. A characteristic dark chocolate-brown discoloration of the stem extends from below the soil line upward, with lesions continuous from the roots up the lower stem.
summary.quote: infected stems appear bruised and are soft, secondary roots are rotted, the leaves turn yellow, and brown and plants can wilt and die... brown lesions form on the roots, the roots rot and degrade, and a dark chocolate-brown discoloration of the stem often extends from below the soil line upward into lower parts of the plant.

diagnostic_features.value: A dark chocolate-brown discoloration of the stem extends from below the soil line upward, with lesions continuous from the roots and up the lower stem. Wilted leaves remain attached to the plant after death. Plants are often killed in patches or in sections of rows.
diagnostic_features.quote: a dark chocolate-brown discoloration of the stem often extends from below the soil line upward into lower parts of the plant. Leaves turn yellow, wilt, and typically stay attached after plant death. Plants are often killed in patches or in sections of rows.

look_alikes.quote: It is important to recognize that Pythium is a related pathogen that causes similar damage and symptoms on seeds and seedlings... This disease can be confused with stem canker, which usually starts at a node and spreads up and down the stems of infected plants.

value: Cultural practices that improve soil drainage to reduce saturated-soil conditions
Seed treatment or soil-applied fungicides containing mefenoxam (Apron XL) or metalaxyl (Apron) at the highest labeled rates
Plant resistant cultivars; the most widely effective resistance genes are Rps 1c and 1k, with Rps 1a, Rps 3, and other genes also effective in some areas
Where Rps genes are overcome by pathogen races, plant cultivars with high field tolerance (partial resistance)
Crop rotation and tillage may provide some benefit
treatments.quote: Try to create a field environment that reduces the likelihood of saturated soil by taking steps to increase drainage where possible. Treatment of seed with the highest labeled rates of fungicidal compounds such as mefenoxam (Apron XL®) or metalaxyl (Apron®) can be beneficial. In all areas where Phytophthora root and stem rot has been a problem, resistant cultivars should be planted. The most common resistance genes that are widely effective are Rps 1c and 1k. Rps1a can be effective, but is not as effective in many areas as the other genes. Rps 3 and other genes can also be effective. For areas where the Rps genes are not working well due to the presence of pathogen races that overcome the resistance, cultivars with high levels of field tolerance (or partial resistance) should be planted. Crop rotation and tillage may be of some benefit.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Sclerotinia stem rot of soybean is caused by the fungus Sclerotinia sclerotiorum .

type_of_disease.quote: Sclerotinia stem rot of soybean is caused by the fungus Sclerotinia sclerotiorum .

affected_parts.quote: ascospores are produced and forcibly ejected into the air where they are spread by wind or splashing rainwater to stems, branches, flowers and pods... mycelia can grow through leaves, stems and petioles... If the seeds were infected during the early stages of development, they may have a flat and shriveled appearance

summary.value: Diseased plants first appear wilted with grayish green leaves that later turn brown to tan; stems develop grayish to bleached white lesions covered by white fluffy mycelia under high moisture, with hard black sclerotia later forming inside or outside stems and pods.
summary.quote: Diseased plants first appear wilted and with grayish green leaves that later turn brown to tan (Figure 2). The stems also have grayish to bleached white lesions that are covered by white fluffy mycelia under conditions of high moisture (Figures 3A and 3B). In later stages, sclerotia can be observed inside or outside of the stems and pods

diagnostic_features.value: Grayish to bleached white stem lesions covered by white fluffy mycelia under high moisture, and the production of hard, black, irregularly shaped sclerotia with a pink to white center inside or outside stems and pods; infected seeds may be flat and shriveled or replaced by sclerotia.
diagnostic_features.quote: The stems also have grayish to bleached white lesions that are covered by white fluffy mycelia under conditions of high moisture... a survival structure called a sclerotium (pl. sclerotia), which is black, hard and irregular in shape with a pink to white center... If the seeds were infected during the early stages of development, they may have a flat and shriveled appearance, or may be replaced by sclerotia.

value: Select cultivars with high levels of partial resistance and an open canopy of upright leaves
Use long crop rotation with non-host crops such as corn and wheat to minimize inoculum build-up over time
Good weed management because several weeds are hosts for the pathogen
Tillage for fields with first occurrence of Sclerotinia stem rot to bury the sclerotia, while reduced tillage is preferred for fields with a long history of the disease
One year of moldboard plowing to bury sclerotia at least 10 centimeters in soil, delaying production of apothecia
Reduction of seeding rate to improve air circulation, reducing humidity and increasing temperature within the soybean canopy
Increasing row spacing to 30 inch rows to avoid complete canopy closure
Chemical control with fungicides (rotation of active ingredients); Boscalid has provided good efficacy in reducing disease incidence and severity
Lactofen herbicide to modify soybean canopy, delay flowering and induce systemic acquired resistance
Biological control with the fungus Coniothyrium minitans to reduce inoculum levels in the field
Avoid introduction of sclerotia in the field; clean seed-lots using spiral separators and gravity tables
Avoid harvesting heavily infested fields until end of season to prevent contamination of combines with sclerotia
Seed treatments with fungicides to treat infected seed
Where irrigation is used, reduce irrigation frequency during flowering
Low plant populations or wide rows may help reduce white mold severity
treatments.quote: Host resistance: There is no complete resistance reported for Sclerotinia stem rot of soybean. However, cultivars with high levels of partial resistance are commercially available... Use of long crop rotation... Corn and wheat can be used in crop rotation with soybean to minimize inoculum build-up over time... Tillage is recommended for fields with first occurrence of Sclerotinia stem rot in order to bury the sclerotia, while reduced tillage is preferred for fields with a long history of the disease... Reduction of seeding rate may improve air circulation... increasing row spacing to 30 inch rows may avoid complete canopy closure... Boscalid has provided good efficacy in reducing disease incidence and severity... Lactofen is an herbicide able to modify soybean canopy, delay flowering and induce systemic acquired resistance response... Biological control with the fungus Coniothyrium minitans has been used in other crops systems... Seed-lots can be cleaned by spiral separators and gravity tables... Seed treatments with fungicides could be used to treat infected seed.

conflicts.canonical_value: Sclerotinia Stem Rot (White Mold) of Soybean
conflicts.note: Same disease, minor wording variation between sources.

conflicts.canonical_value: Foliar, Stem, Pod, Seed, Whole plant
conflicts.note: UMN does not list foliar/leaf involvement explicitly; Ohioline includes leaves and petioles.
